CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 58
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
NCR 1 Dated 29 May 2019
Standard Reference: Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standard v. 3.1
Document Reference: Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (3-22-
19).pdf
Finding: The CCB Standard Indicator G1.10 requires that the project: “Identify likely natural and
human-induced risk to the expected climate, community and biodiversity benefits during the project
lifetime and outline measures needed and taken to mitigate the risk”, where human-induced risks are
defined as “including risks in the shorter and longer term, risk related to continued community
willingness to participate in the project, risks related to ability to adapt to climate change and climate
variability, etc.”
In the course of the site visit, the verification team spoke with members of the community as well as
project staff and heard of regions within the project area that were possibly subject to active
deforestation, including, 1) an area in the southeast portion said to be accessed by a small river
channel, and, 2) an area in the northern portion said to be accessible by new road running from
highway BR 364. Both areas appear on the maps to be deforested with sizable clearings and openings.
The MR documents the use of informal monitoring/forest patrols along the Jurupari River. Section
2.2.1, for instance, states, for the period of 2012-present: “With respect to initiating patrols of
deforestation, the Project Proponents have reviewed the satellite imagery from 2012 to 2018. Mazinho
and Francisco also began informal monitoring via boat with a focus on the Jurupari River.” However,
the MR does not appear to identify the types of risk referenced above relative to deforestation occurring
within the project area but not along the Jurupari River, or outline measures needed and taken to
mitigate such risks.
Project Personnel Response: The original plan was for Mazinho to visit both the ramal and the
igarape in June 2019; however, Mazinho was injured in a motorcycle accident. Mazinho is now
recovering and doing better. The revised plan is for Mazinho to visit both the ramal and the igarape the
week of July 22, 2019. Mazinho will take pictures and videos of the areas deforested. In addition,
Mazinho will inform those responsible for the deforestation that beyond their areas is a private property
doing a conservation project and deforestation is not allowed. If Mazinho is able to identify the people
responsible for deforesting, JR Agropecuária e Empreendimentos EIRELI will follow the proper legal
process by contacting IBAMA. More specifically, a compliant would be filed with IBAMA, IBAMA would
check by their means to see if the complaint is legitimate and if the complaint is valid, IBAMA would
then try to visit the area, and IBAMA would levy fines if necessary. Alternatively, the landowner could
also file a “Ownership Return” paperwork with the State Prosecutor and this would be a formal way to
have people removed from the area by the police department. This process is comparable to an
eviction notice. As of right now, there does not appear to be deforestation within the Project Area. This
information will be added to the Monitoring Report.
Auditor Response: The audit team reviewed the text that has been added to Section 2.2.6 Risks to
the Project of the revised Monitoring Report ("Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018
Monitoring Report (8-02-19) - TRACKED") and agrees that the risks are properly identified and the
measures "needed and taken" to mitigate the risks are appropriate and well-described, per the
requirements of CCB Indicator G1.10. The finding is closed.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 59
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
NCR 2 Dated 29 May 2019
Standard Reference: Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standard v. 3.1
Document Reference: Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (3-22-
19).pdf
Finding: The CCB Standard Indicator G3.4 requires that projects: “Describe how communities
including all the community groups and other stakeholders have influenced project design and
implementation through effective consultation, particularly with a view to optimizing community and
other stakeholder benefits, respecting local customs, values and institutions and maintaining high
conservation values. Projects proponents must document consultation and indicate if and how the
project design and implementation has been revised based on such input. A plan must be developed
and implemented to continue communication and consultation between the project proponents and
communities, including all the community groups, and other stakeholder about the project and its
impact to facilitate adaptive management throughout the life of the project.”
The MR Section 2.3.8 described the plan for continued communication as follows; “The Project
Proponents aim to collectively meet with the local communities at least once per year. This includes
visits in December 2014, November 2015, January 2017, and May 2018. The local project manager
Mazinho meets with the local communities several times a year. Furthermore, the Project Proponents
also meet with Other Stakeholders, particularly in Rio Branco, approximately 1-3 times per year.”
During the site visit, interviews were conducted with the majority of families living within the project area
listed as community members; a common area of feedback was that most of what was understood
about the project, as well as opportunities for consultation, came from the visit conducted in 2014 by
Ayri Rando. The verification team was told that subsequent visits were smaller in scope, number of
community members involved, and were held for varying purposes. While the verification team
understands that the plan is for different members of the project team to visit at a frequency of at least
once per year, the plan should also detail what type of communication and consultation will occur
during planned visits.
Project Personnel Response: CarbonCo has added information to the monitoring report about the
type of communication and consultation this is planned for the next visit in May 2020.
In sum, the communities would like another in-depth explanation about the Project and this will take
place in May 2020. The Project Proponents may do this in-depth explanation themselves with the
community. In addition, CarbonCo will explore the possibility of rehiring Ayri Rando (the independent
community specialist that was hired back in 2014) to revisit the communities. CarbonCo emailed Ayri
Rando on July 24, 2019 to see about this possibility. In addition, the Project Proponents will look into
hiring someone from Rio Branco that knows a lot about community development and REDD+. This
might include someone from IMAC, IMC, SOS Amazonia, and/or STR-Feijo.
Auditor Response: The audit team reviewed the text that has been added to Section 2.3.8 Continued
Consultation and Adaptive Management of the revised Monitoring Report ("Envira Amazonia Project's
CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (8-02-19) - TRACKED") and agrees that the measures
described and documented constitute an appropriate plan to continue communication and consultation
between the project proponents and the communities regarding the project, per requirements of CCB
Indicator G3.4.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 60
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
NCR 3 Dated 29 May 2019
Standard Reference: Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standard v. 3.1
Document Reference: Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (3-22-
19).pdf
Finding: The CCB Standard Indicator G3.7 requires that the project: “Describe the measures needed
and taken to ensure that the project proponent and all other entities involved in project design and
implementation are not involved in or complicit in any form of discrimination or sexual harassment with
respect to the project.” Indicator G3.11 states “Submit a list of all relevant laws and regulations
covering worker’s rights in the host country. Describe measures needed and taken to inform workers
about their rights. Provide assurance that the project meets or exceeds all applicable laws and/or
regulations covering worker rights and, where relevant, demonstrate how compliance is achieved.”
While on the site visit, the audit team heard of no discriminatory act nor any acts related to sexual
harassment related to the project, nor against any members living in the project area associated with
the project. This finding related to information in the Monitoring Report. In particular, Section 2.3.11 of
the project’s MR discusses the project’s policy towards anti-discrimination in general but lacks
specificity regarding measures needed and taken to ensure that the project proponent and all other
entities involved in project design and implementation are not involved in or complicit in any form of
discrimination or sexual harassment with respect to the project. In addition, Section 2.3.15 outlines
applicable laws and regulations but does not reference a law regarding sexual harassment, called Lei
No. 8.112, de 1990, which the verification team believes to be relevant.
Project Personnel Response: With respect to the assurance that Project Proponents have never
acted with discrimination or acts of sexual harassment against members living in the Project Area, what
can be explained is throughout all these years of project development, there have been no complaints
in this regard. There were numerous visits made to the Project by the Project Proponents to meet with
the community and these meetings always took place in a respectful and cordial manner, with no
disrespect on both sides. The Brazilian law related to sexual harassment has been added to the
Monitoring Report.
There is no formal employment relationship with the residents of the community that reside in the
Project. With the exception of Cirlandio and Mazinho who work on a temporary basis and with
intermittent contracts of service that are provided for in labor legislation. Whenever the Project
Proponents require the services of Cirlandio and Mazinho, they are remunerated in proportion to the
work performed as planned.
Auditor Response: The audit team reiterates that it heard of no discriminatory acts associated with the
project while on the site visit or otherwise.
The audit team reviewed the text that has been added to Section 2.3.15 Relevant Laws and
Regulations Related to Worker’s Rights, and to Section 2.3.11 Anti-Discrimination Assurance of the
revised Monitoring Report ("Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (8-02-
19) - TRACKED") and agrees that the requested information has been added, per requirement of CCB
Indicator G3.7. The finding is closed.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 61
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
NCR 4 Dated 29 May 2019
Standard Reference: Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standard v. 3.1
Document Reference: Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (3-22-
19).pdf
Finding: The CCB Standard Indicator G3.8 relates to the project’s Feedback and Grievance Redress
Procedure (FGRP) and requires the project: “Demonstrate that a clear grievance redress procedure
has been formalized to address disputes with communities and other stakeholders that may arise
during project planning, implementation and evaluation with respect but not limited to, free, prior and
informed consent, rights to lands, territories and resources, benefit sharing and participation”
Section 2.3.12 of the project MR details the project’s FGRP, in which the State of Acre’s Climate
Change Institute plays an important role. However, given the information that the Institute was recently
dissolved, the FGRP requires revision. In addition, while on the site visit, based on site visit
observations and interviews, an improvement in the formalized process by which community members
can raise issues with the Project is needed to comply with the referenced Indicator.
Project Personnel Response: The Climate Change Institute of Acre has been reinstated and will
continue to serve as the ombudsman for the Envira Amazonia Project. The new information with the
Climate Change Institute (i.e., website, new address, etc.) has been added to the Monitoring Report.
Auditor Response: The audit team reviewed the revised Section 2.3.12 regarding the Climate Change
Institute's dissolution and subsequent reinstallment in the revised Monitoring Report ("Envira Amazonia
Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (8-02-19) - TRACKED") and confirmed that the
information provided is sufficient. With regard to community feedback mechanisms being
formalized/improved, the audit team believes the information provided in NCR.2 regarding consultation
in 2020 is sufficient to address the finding. The finding is closed.
OBS 5 Dated 29 May 2019
Standard Reference: Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standard v. 3.1
Document Reference: Envira Amazonia Project's CCB VCS 2016-2018 Monitoring Report (3-22-
19).pdf
Finding: The CCB Indicator G3.10 states as follows “Demonstrate that people from the communities
are given an equal opportunity to fill all work positions (including management) if the job requirements
are met. Explain how workers are selected for positions and where relevant, describe the measures
needed and taken to ensure community members, including women and vulnerable and/or
marginalized people, are given a fair chance to fill positions for which they can be trained.”
It was brought to the attention of the verification team that several community members felt that the
number of families chosen to work for the project was too narrow. It was expressed that, of the small
number of people chosen to be employed in short term positions by the project, many were members
of the same family (e.g. husband-wife, father-son), such that a smaller number of potential families
benefit economically then was possible.
Project Personnel Response: The Project Proponents have hired two main, temporary employees
Mazinho and Circlandio who are from different families. Going forward, the Project Proponents will
strive to diversify those hired to assist with project activities such as with the bird monitoring study
scheduled for May 2020.
Bearing on Material Misstatement or Conformance (M/C/NA): NA

4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Iniciamos a elaboração de nossos Inventários de Emissões de Gases de Efeito Estufa em 2021, contabilizando as
emissões diretas e indiretas de nossas atividades retroativas aos anos de 2018 a 2020, com o intuito de entender o
histórico de evolução de nossa pegada ambiental. Para dar maior transparência em relação aos nossos
compromissos e, para atender ao nosso Plano de Ação Institucional ESG, ingressamos em 2021 na plataforma do
Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol com a publicação de nossos inventários no Registro Público de Emissões (RPE).
Temos como principais características o uso de ferramentas de tecnologias, bem como uma estrutura de
atendimento de gerentes comerciais, no relacionamento com os clientes que não requerem agências físicas, o que
diminui nosso impacto com relação às emissões de gases de efeito estufa. Outro fator importante é que nossa
infraestrutura em nuvem permite também alcançar maior eficiência nas operações.
Nossa operação é amparada pelos regimes de trabalho home-office e híbrido, além do presencial, adotados desde
2020, a depender da necessidade de cada colaborador. Identificamos uma diminuição considerável das emissões
de maior relevância de nossa pegada, que são as relacionadas ao Escopo 3, Categoria 7 (Deslocamento de
funcionários "casa-trabalho"), influenciadas pela adoção estes novos modelos de trabalho.
Desde 2021, adotamos uma metodologia interna para análise comparativa de nossas emissões equivalentes de
CO2 totais por colaborador em relação a outros bancos de mesmo porte e maior porte que publicaram as suas
emissões referentes ao ano 2020 no RPE, para entendermos onde estaríamos posicionados com relação às nossas
emissões no setor financeiro.
Comparativamente ao ano de 2021, houve um aumento de 14% das emissões totais resultante principalmente do
aumento no quadro de colaboradores na ordem de 19%. Dessa forma, houve um aumento da maioria dos
indicadores que compõem o cálculo de emissões dos Escopos 1, 2 e 3 dando destaque aos indicadores
relacionados ao Escopo 3 (emissões indiretas), nosso maior ofensor. Cabe destacar que, a manutenção do ar-
condicionado com gás refrigerante dentro do Escopo 1 refletiu em redução na ordem de 66%, amparada pela troca
dos modelos mais novos de aparelhos no prédio JK360, local de nossa sede até julho/2022.
Em agosto/2022 oficializamos nossa mudança para uma nova sede administrativa, o Eldorado Business Tower,
edifício com Certificação Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) nível Platinum, que representa o
mais alto grau de performance ambiental em suas operações, alinhadas às melhores práticas internacionais de
sustentabilidade na construção civil.
Por fim, cabe destacar que nossas emissões relativas por colaborador ficaram em 0,76 toneladas de
CO2eq/colaborador, uma diminuição de 4% em relação a 2021, mesmo com o aumento das contratações.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
No Banco Fibra, monitoramos ações dentro do plano de neutralização de emissões, priorizando o
acompanhamento do Escopo e da Categoria que contemplam a fonte de maior emissão de Gases de Efeitos Estufa
(GEE), no contexto da nossa instituição: o Escopo 3, Categoria 7 (Deslocamento de funcionários "casa-trabalho").
28/09/2023 13:17 10
Inventário 2022 - Banco Fibra
Dentro disso, nosso indicador consiste em Emissões (tCO2eq)/ colaborador/ ano.
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
Nosso Plano de Ação Institucional ESG, estabelecido desde 2021 e periodicamente revisado e aprovado no âmbito
de nosso Conselho de Administração, contempla ações de curto e médio prazo, no qual consideramos o pilar
ambiental como parte essencial na gestão sustentável de nossos negócios. Em 2021, além do reporte no
Repositório Público de Emissões do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol, realizamos nossa primeira avaliação
independente do processo, garantindo obtenção do Selo Ouro, ação que demonstra o atendimento de todos os
critérios de transparência na publicação de seu inventário de gases de efeito estufa.
Além disso, mantemos desde 2021 um dashboard disponibilizado a todos os colaboradores em nossa intranet e
que contempla indicadores relacionados a nossas emissões diretas e indiretas, atualizado e monitorado
mensalmente. Mantivemos também incentivo para alternativas de deslocamento de nossos colaboradores através
de um modal menos poluente, por meio de parceria com empresa de bicicletas elétricas para que nossos
colaboradores tivessem desconto no aluguel.
Para encerrar o primeiro semestre de 2022, em celebração à Semana do Meio Ambiente, e, como forma de engajar
nossos colaboradores a promover ações voltadas à sustentabilidade, lançamos uma campanha estimulando-os a
enviarem fotos ou vídeos relacionados às suas contribuições ao meio ambiente e que foram publicados em nossa
Intranet, aliadas às comunicações para a conscientização sobre o descarte correto de resíduos recicláveis e não
recicláveis. Além disso, nossas comunicações corporativas em 2022 contemplaram diversos temas relacionados à
dimensão ambiental.
Atualizamos em dezembro/2022 nossa política de gerenciamento de resíduos, a qual institucionaliza este processo
para todos os nossos colaboradores. Seguiremos com ações que visem não apenas a compensação como também
redução de nossas emissões.
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventários de GEE e/ou ao envio de informações relacionadas.
Todas as nossas minutas contratuais contemplam cláusulas com clientes e fornecedores, que visam o cumprimento
de obrigações socioambientais de respeito ao meio ambiente. Todos os clientes que realizam operações de crédito
com o Banco, são avaliados sob o prisma dos riscos sociais, ambientais e climáticos, recebendo um rating acerca
destes elementos denominado Rating Rsac, cuja classificação ampara, inclusive, a aceitação de clientes no âmbito
do Comitê de Crédito. Há também a obrigatoriedade de apresentação do Questionário Social, Ambiental e Climático
(QSAC) no cadastro ou renovação de clientes que realizarem operações de crédito, cujo faturamento seja acima de
R$ 60 milhões, bem como fornecedores classificados como relevantes para o Banco. Neste Questionário são
solicitadas informações referentes à diligências sociais, ambientais e climáticas do cliente/fornecedor, incluindo
questões relativas à certificação ISO 14.064, sobre Gestão de Emissões de Gases do Efeito Estufa (GEE), bem como
se o cliente/fornecedor adota boas práticas relacionadas à redução da emissão de gases de efeito estufa como
forma de prevenção de poluição inerentes às suas atividades. Os critérios de aplicação do QSAC constam em nossa
Política de Responsabilidade Social, Ambiental e Climática (PRSAC), disponível em nosso site institucional.
4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
Para o inventário referente ao ano de 2022 foram consideradas todos as fontes de dados
disponíveis do Banco Fibra. Foram também aplicados modelos de geolocalização, pesquisa sobre modal de

transporte utilizado nos dias de trabalho e base de acessos à catraca do prédio administrativo, para estimar os

("casa-trabalho")) pertencente ao Escopo 3.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
A elaboração do inventário do ano de 2022 foi feita por consultoria especializada na quantificação de emissões de
gases de efeito estufa através da utilização da metodologia do GHG Protocol, a partir da coleta e consolidação de
dados e informações do Banco Fibra. Aprimoramos a coleta de dados do indicador “Deslocamento Casa-Trabalho”

de transporte utilizado no deslocamento ao trabalho e outra com informações de frequência de acesso de nossos
colaboradores a partir da catraca do prédio administrativo. Para o cálculo final de deslocamento dos colaboradores,
foram aplicadas técnicas de analytics com automação em linguagem phyton.
Realizamos a contratação de verificação externa para acreditar nosso inventário de emissões de gases de efeito
estufa referente às atividades do ano de 2022.

1 PROJECT DETAILS
1.2 Summary Description of the Implementation Status of the Project
The UNITOR REDD+ PROJECT is located in Lábrea, Amazonas State, Brazil, which is the municipality
with the fourth highest aggregate deforestation rate in Brazil between 2008 – 2020, according to
PRODES data1. Municipal deforestation rates have risen year on year, from 3.8% in 2017 to 5.3% in
2020, making this a priority area for forest conservation worldwide. A new consortium of 15
neighbouring rural properties comprises the Unitor REDD+ Project area, together covering 99,035.20
ha of forest area, to develop forest carbon activities under the guidance and inspiration of the nearby
Fortaleza Ituxi REDD Project. The main Project activities that have been implemented during this first
monitoring period are listed below with their respective dates, for more detailed on the implementation
of each Project Activity see Section 3.1.
1. Intensify and improve the efficiency of surveillance actions: patrolling of the proprieties was
already carried out before the REDD+ project, but since 2018 it has been improving and
intensifying, to be continued throughout the project lifetime. The patrolling is done by land,
air and river, inhibiting illegal invasion and quickly detecting fires and invasions.
2. Construction of 3 monitoring posts: Three bases were built before start date of the project
(Santa Maria, São José I, and Santa Luzia). Another 3 bases were built in São Manoel, Três
Barras and São José, in July 2019, July 2020 and July 2021, respectively.
3. Acquisition of vehicles for ground patrols: A motorcycle was purchased in April 2020, two
pickups in September 2020, and another pickup in June 2021. The vehicles are used for
monitoring and for the mobility of workers.
4. Fire brigade training: the first training was held on March 9, 2019
5. Forest management: The management were carried out at Três Barras, Santa Catarina,
Presidente Prudente and São Manoel farms between 2019 and July 2021, as described in
Table 8. A training about good forest management practices was also carried out with the
teams between September 1st and 2nd, 2020.
The activities developed so far play a fundamental role in minimizing deforestation and curbing
invasions, bringing benefits for climate and biodiversity. Future activities will be developed to
engage and benefit residents close to the project, such as training in livestock and sustainable
forest management, in addition to workshops about health and well-being.
The net GHG emission reductions for this monitoring period are estimated to be 2,282,826
tCO2e.

1.6 Project Start Date
The Unitor Project’s Start Date is defined as the date of implementation of the first Sustainable Forest
Management (SFMP) activities on the ground. The first property to implement its SFMP was the Santa
Maria on 26/04/2018. Various instances of the project became active on different dates, as described
in the VCS PD.
SFMPs have the explicit intention of contributing to conservation and to atmospheric carbon
sequestration, they are key to forest surveillance by providing personnel on the ground. Furthermore,
critically, SFMPs are an explicit component of the Brazilian National REDD+ Strategy2.Therefore, the
initiation of the respective SFMP activities are defined as the Project Start Dates.

1.8 Project Location
The UNITOR REDD+ PROJECT consists of a 99,035.20 hectares total Project Area of protected
native forests, located in a region with the fourth highest deforestation rate in the Amazon Biome3
at the time of writing: the municipality of Lábrea. Being one of the southernmost municipalities
in the Brazilian state of Amazonas, Lábrea is geographically located close to the “Arc of
Deforestation” and borders with other highly deforested municipalities to the South (Rio Branco,
State of Acre; and Porto Velho, State of Rondônia)

The project neither has nor intends to generate any other form of GHG-related environmental
credit for GHG emission reductions or removals claimed under the VCS Program. The project has
not sought nor received another form of GHG-related environmental credit, including renewable
energy certificates.
1.12 Sustainable Development
The map below shows the strategic zone for “Containment of the expansion fronts with protected
areas and alternative uses”, which was established by the MacroZEE/AL (Macrozoneamento
Ecológico-Econômico da Amazônia Legal; Ecological and Economic Macro-zoning of Amazon)
developed by the Brazilian Ministry of Environment6, which encompasses the Project Area. The
MacroZEE/AL aims to establish strategic indications of occupation and use of land on a
sustainable basis to guide, at the regional scale, the development and spatial distribution of
public development policies, territorial and environmental planning, as well as the decisions of
private agents. Due to its shield function for the heart forest protection, this territorial unit
deserves strengthening policies. In this context, the Unitor REDD+ Project aligns with the
strategies set up by the MacroZEE/AL of the Brazilian Ministry of Environment.
6 Source MMA (accessed 23/03/21): https://antigo.mma.gov.br/component/k2/item/8200-figuras
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
Figure 6. Containment of the expansion fronts with protected areas and alternative uses (Source:
Brazilian Ministry of the Environment – MMA)
Due to the increase in deforestation in the Legal Amazon, the Action Plan for the Prevention and
Control of Deforestation in the Legal Amazon (PPCDAm: Plano de Ação para a Prevenção e
Controle do Desmatamento na Amazônia Legal) came into effect, starting mitigation and ongoing
actions to reduce deforestation. The Unitor REDD+ Project is in line with main PPCDAm
premises7.
At the international level, the Unitor REDD+ Project complies with the logic of the environmental
priorities defined by the Brazilian Federal Administration at COP 14 in December 2008, where a
deforestation reduction goal of 70% by the year 2018 was declared, and following that, further
goals of achieving zero illegal deforestation by 2030, and offsetting of greenhouse gas emissions
originating from legal removal of vegetation. The latter are elements of the Brazilian NDC, which
the country aims to adopt more firmly within the framework of the Paris Climate Accords (COP-
21)8. To attain these goals, an essential component will be to ally government initiatives with
independent actions such as that proposed under the present project.
Going into more detail, the Unitor project is aligned with Brazil’s commitments to the UN
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Firstly, on the environmental side, Brazil has made

programas/ppa/plano-plurianual-ppa-2016-2019-1/relatorio_avaliacao_programa_2050-mudanca_do_clima.pdf
8 Source MMA (accessed 19/04/21): http://redd.mma.gov.br/pt/redd-e-a-indc-brasileira
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
commitments to SDG 13 – “Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts” –
aligning its national goals9 with the UN’s, for example UN goal 13.310:
• 13.3: Improve education, increase human and institutional awareness and capacity on
climate change, its risks, mitigation, adaptation, impacts, and early warning systems.
The project activities involving technical qualification, training in forest management, and
community development in the form of participative workshops, aim to increase the collective
understanding of climate change and the importance of the forest. This intervention at the small-
scale level has broader objectives, for a collective transformation of cultural relations lifestyles
of the local community and the economy, in its relation to the environment.
Regarding goal 13, the Unitor REDD project involves the conservation of a predicted 24,031.3
hectares of forest over its lifespan and it is an essential activity for climate change mitigation.
Perhaps most importantly, the project is aligned with SDG 15 regarding Terrestrial Ecosystems,
which was adopted into the Brazilian goals as follows: “15.1. By 2020, to conserve at least 30%
of the Amazon, through systems of conservation units provided for in the Law on the National
System of Conservation Units (SNUC), and other categories of officially protected areas such as
Permanent Preservation Areas (APPs), Legal Reserves (RLs) and indigenous lands with native
vegetation (…)”. The present project’s direct conservation of APPs and RLs, as well as synergistic
actions with the surrounding Conservation Units are strongly aligned with this national goal.
Brazilian SDG goal 15.2, regarding sustainable forest management, tree plantation and
restoration of forests, which stipulates to “eliminate illegal deforestation in all Brazilian biomes
by 2030, expand the area of forests under sustainable environmental management and recover
12 million hectares of degraded forests and other forms of native vegetation, in all biomes and
preferably in Permanent Preservation Areas (APPs) and Legal Reserves (RLs) and, in areas of
alternative land use, expand the planted forest area by 1.4 million hectares”, is directly supported
by the Unitor project’s following activities:
- Sustainable forest management within seven of the properties;
- Monitoring of illegal land occupation, illegal logging, and illegal deforestation: 6
monitoring bases have been installed so far, to serve as housing and support for the
officers who carry out patrols. Ground patrols are carried out periodically with vehicles
(motorcycles and pickup trucks) or on foot. River monitoring is usually done twice a week,
and air monitoring is usually done once a month. Monitoring frequencies may change,
especially in the rainy season when access is limited. Besides that,
- Ultimate goals of avoided deforestation of 24,769 hectares of Amazon rainforest over 30
years on private lands, preserving APPs and RLs.

Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
On the socio-economic front, Brazil adopted “UN Goal 1: End poverty in all its forms
everywhere”11, “UN Goal 3: Ensure a healthy life and promote well-being for everyone, at all ages”,
and “UN Goal 8: Promote inclusive and sustainable economic growth, employment and decent
work for all”, and, with the most relevant Brazilian sub-goals to the project being:
- SDG 1.1: “By 2030, eradicate extreme poverty for all people everywhere, currently
measured as people living on less than PPC$3.20 a day”;
- SDG 1.2: “By 2030, reduce proportion of men, women and children of all ages living in
monetary and non-monetary poverty by at least half, in all its dimensions, according to
national definitions”;
- SDG 3.4: “By 2030, reduce premature mortality from non-communicable diseases by
one third through prevention and treatment, and promote mental health and well-being”
- SDG 8.3: “Promote development with the generation of decent work; formalization; the
growth of micro, small and medium companies; entrepreneurship and innovation”; and
- SDG 8.4: “Increase the efficiency of the use of global resources in consumption and
production, and strive to decouple economic growth from environmental degradation, in
accordance with the Action Plan for Sustainable Production and Consumption (PPCS).”
In line with objective 8.3, during the monitoring period of this report, 10 people were employed
to carry out various activities, such as patrolling, forest management and general services. In
addition, the project is expected to generate at least 40 job openings throughout the project
period, including permanent and temporary ones:
- At least two forest monitors per each of the five deforestation monitoring bases, totaling
ten jobs (SDG 1.1);
- An average of five team members per each of the six sustainable forest management
areas, which are temporary positions, with an unpredictable number of total job
vacancies for separate individuals, estimated at 30 jobs (SDG 1.1);
- Equal opportunities relating to gender in the project indicators will be monitored
(SDG1.2);
- The organization of training events focusing on sustainable cattle ranching and forest
management will be offered after the validation of the project and sales of credits (SDG
8.3 and 8.4).
This project therefore represents enormous potential to continue the work started by the
neighboring Fortaleza Ituxi REDD Project: assisting the Federal Administration and state agencies
to attain these national- and international-level goals and leverage further REDD projects,
providing mitigation for these municipalities which are facing critical deforestation levels, as in
the case for Lábrea, Nova Califórnia, and Extrema.

2 SAFEGUARDS
2.1 No Net Harm
The project activities do not cause any potential negative offsite stakeholder impacts. The project
does not result in net negative impacts on the wellbeing of other stakeholder groups. A
communication channel was established for stakeholders to express their concerns and to solve
potential conflicts and grievances that arise during project planning and implementation. The
main communication channel is the project's own email
(reddunitorcarbonext@carbonext.com.br), which is managed by the Carbonext team. But the
residents close to the project also have the Whatsapp contact for the owners and field managers
and can send messages directly to them in case of conflicts, questions or suggestions. During
the period of this monitoring report, no complaints or denunciations were made by the
communities.
Furthermore, technical qualification may increase the collective understanding of climate change
and the importance of the forest. This understanding is essential for each individual in the
process of a collective transformation of cultural relations and of the lifestyle of the local
community. Training on sustainable livestock and forest management will be carried out after the
validation of the project and the sales of credits.
Aiming at reducing the risk of forest fires, on March 9, 2019, a training session was carried out
with 6 employees for the formation of a fire brigade. Topics on prevention and control of forest
fires were addressed. This training will be conducted annually, after the validation and
verification of the project.
In addition, between September 1st and 2nd, 2020, a training was carried out with the forest
management teams with the objective of disseminating good management practices in order to
reduce the impacts of forest exploitation. Furthermore, it was explained about the laws and
normative instructions related to forest management practices.
These activities are initiatives to improve and make forestry practices more sustainable and
mitigate potential any environmental impacts.

2.3 AFOLU-Specific Safeguards
During the monitoring period, the project proponent has communicated and consulted neighbour
landowners and wild-harvesters about the development of the Unitor REDD+ Project. This
communication was made in person by the field agents, bringing together groups at the project's
monitoring bases to realize a meeting or visiting people's homes.
During the public consultation, participants were informed about project details such as location,
objectives and activities. After the explanation, all participants signed the document “Declaration
of Consent with the Project”, where they give their free consent and acceptance with the project
objectives and its development. In addition, given that the project is carried out in a private
territory, there are no conflicts over title with the surrounding communities.
A communication channel was established with them in order to give necessary support and
mitigate any possible risk due to the project implementation. As mentioned above, the ongoing
communication with the neighbours and communities is made through project’s email
(reddunitorcarbonext@carbonext.com.br) and also through the field monitors, who are often in
the project area and are accessible to chat in person or through WhatsApp. In case of complaints
or denunciations, the grievance redress procedure will follow as below:
1. The Project Proponent shall attempt to amicably resolve all grievances and provide a
written response to the grievances in a manner that is culturally appropriate. (Action must
be taken in 7 days);
2. Any grievances that are not resolved by amicable negotiations shall be referred to
mediation by a neutral third party. (Action must be taken in 30 days);
3. Any grievances that are not resolved through mediation shall be referred either to a)
arbitration, to the extent allowed by the laws of the relevant jurisdiction or b) competent
courts in the relevant jurisdiction, without prejudice to a party’s ability to submit the
grievance to a competent supranational adjudicatory body, if any. (The time to accomplish
this stage is dependent on local jurisdiction delays.)
However, during the public consultation, participants did not identify any conflicts or risks
caused by the project to their interests and well-being. And at the timing of the writing of this
report, no complaints or suggestions have been made by neighbors or communities, thus, it is
possible to conclude that there were no negative impacts on the property and land use rights
of the local stakeholders during the period of this MR.
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0

Leakage will be monitored annually through analysis of satellite images and maps. The remote sensing
is carried out according to the following plan:
1) Land use and land cover mapping is assessed using images with spatial resolution superior to
30 meters. Image acquisition is performed during the period of low incidence of clouds and
rainfall in the region, within July and September.
2) Images undergo geometric correction by means of geo-referencing, using topographic maps as
reference or USG-NASA orthorectified images.
3) For analysis of areas with cloud cover, visual interpretation of radar image is performed.
4) Evaluation of classification accuracy is performed by analysing the overall accuracy and kappa
index obtained from a confusion matrix. The minimum accuracy of the classification mapping
should be 80%.
As described in section 1.17 of the VCS-PD, the leakage management activities of the project are as
follows:
• Technical training on sustainable cattle raising: the objective of this initiative will be qualifying a
labour force that finds little opportunity to work in the region and ends up taking part in illegal
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
settlements and land occupation. It is expected that this initiative will benefit 15 students every
year. Students and technicians of both sexes will be eligible for registrations, with the previous
requirement of having finished the basic studies (middle school). This action is dependent on
sale of credits and therefore has not been carried out yet, as described in section 3.1, Table 6.
• Forest management courses: Courses on forest management methods will be offered to the
internal workers for updating on techniques and practices and to train new employees. The
standardization procedure for these trainings is available for auditing. This action was carried out
in 2020, as described in section 3.1, Table 6.
• Training of fire brigades: training will be given to prepare fire brigade members, and in addition
to the employees, the project’s neighbours will be able to participate in the training event as well.
This action was carried in March 2019, as described in section 3.1, Table 6.
• Support of SEMA – AM’s activities (Secretaria de Estado do Meio Ambiente do Amazonas;
Amazonas State Secretariat of Environment): SEMA will benefit from having, under its jurisdiction,
a second example of an innovative REDD model – the first being the nearby Fortaleza Ituxi REDD
Project, also developed by Carbonext – that can be further replicated on other properties. It will
continue to raise the public profile of the current administration, providing visibility and
methodological advances in environmental preservation. This action is dependent on sale of
credits and therefore has not been carried out yet.
• Surveillance activities designed to mitigate illegal logging and land occupation in the area will be
achieved through the Unitor REDD+ Project, summing with the efforts of the Fortaleza Ituxi REDD
Project, thus covering a significant area of Lábrea municipality. By continuing to promote an
increase in the number of REDD Projects in the region, whenever feasible, as well as sustainable
forest management plans, the project aims to impact deforestation and degradation significantly.
This process will be further consolidated through combined efforts with private and governmental
entities, and NGOs. In this context, the project proponent will spearhead political mobilization of
the regional forest sector and, continue to reinforce the engagement of all sectors involved in
deforestation issues, in the long term. The main condition for execution of this activity is the
approval and validation of the Unitor REDD+ Project, which will be a further important benchmark
for engagement of all potential private landowners in the Lábrea region. As shown in Table 6, 3
bases were constructed, a 3 pickup truckes and 1 motorbike were purchased during the
monitoring period.
• Potential Roll-out to Other Areas: Unitor already being the project proponent’s second REDD
activity in the region, further areas with potential for REDD projects have already been identified
around the project site. At the time of this MR’s publishing, one of the project owners of Unitor is
developing another VCS-REDD project, which is not yet publicly available.
• Combatting illegal land occupation: The local community will be strategic in monitoring illegal
land occupation and potential illegal logging. Those who are interested in being trained and
carrying out local monitoring will be included in the project, an activity which may also become a
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
new source of income for local communities. This action is dependent on sale of credits and
therefore has not been carried out yet.
Regarding the monitoring of the Non-permanence risk factors, they will be monitored through remote
sensing, literature data, and internal Carbonext processes, in accordance with the Non-Permanence
risk tool, completed at each monitoring event12 approach being as follows for each risk category:
Internal risks: Carbonext internal control of project management methods and team according to
adaptive management plan13; Financial Viability assessed through contact with project proprietors;
opportunity cost assessed through financial modelling according to AFOLU Non-Permanence tool.
External risks assessed as per description in the VCS-Non-Permanence-Risk-Report-Template, broadly
summarized below: Land-tenure and Resource Access/Impacts assessed according to contact with
project owners and repeated stakeholder consultations; Community Engagement addressed via
stakeholder consultation, which is repeated at each MR; and Political Risk is subject to a desk-based
assessment according to AFOLU Non-Permanence Risk Tool.
Natural risks assessed via analysis of satellite images and maps, according to the VCS-Non-
Permanence-Risk-Report-Template repeated at each MR. The remote sensing is carried out according
to the following plan:
1) Land use and land cover mapping is assessed using images with spatial resolution superior to
30 meters. Image acquisition is performed during the period of low incidence of clouds and
rainfall in the region, within July and September.
2) Images undergo geometric correction by means of geo-referencing, using topographic maps as
reference or USG-NASA orthorectified images.
3) For analysis of areas with cloud cover, visual interpretation of radar image is performed.
4) Evaluation of classification accuracy is performed by analysing the overall accuracy and kappa
index obtained from a confusion matrix. The minimum accuracy of the classification mapping
should be 80%.
3.3 Deviations

Justification of choice of If deforestation agents do not participate in leakage prevention
data or description of activities and project activities, the Displacement Factor shall be
measurement methods 100%. Where leakage prevention activities are implemented, the
and procedures applied factor shall be equal to the proportion of the baseline agents
estimated to be given the opportunity to participate in leakage
prevention activities and project activities. The project design
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
team estimates that 100% of potential deforestation agents in
the Reference Region will be given the opportunity to participate
in leakage prevention activities.
Projections of deforestation within the Leakage Belt have duly
been made as part of the present project, according to
projections of deforestation presented in Section 4.3 of the PD,
in the Erro! Fonte de referência não encontrada. The project
proponent has conservatively allowed 5% leeway on this
estimate, which is unlikely to be surpassed. If this allowance is
surpassed, an assessment of how much is due to the project will
be conducted during monitoring. Thus, the “Displacement
Leakage Factor” (DLF) was conservatively defined as 0.05. It is
worthy to notice that the project proponent is continuously
divulging and recruiting new landowners to participate in this
grouped REDD initiative, which can be also considered as a
measure to fight leakage. Other leakage fighting activities are
also listed in this VCS-PD, as fire brigade trainings, sustainable
cattle raising etc.

Purpose of data • Calculation of leakage
Calculation method Analysis of satellite images and maps
Where strong evidence can be collected that deforestation in the
Comments
leakage belt is attributable to deforestation agents that are not
linked to the project area, the detected deforestation will not be
attributed to the project activity, thus not considered leakage. As
shown in calculations of this MR, a strong increase in
deforestation has been observed in the entire Reference Region
ex-post. Thus, it has been considered that the conjunctural
increase of deforestation in the Reference Region (including the
Leakage Belt) could not be attributed to this project activity, as a
general change in deforestation trends has been established in
the project region after the project start date, far above the initial
ex-ante deforestation estimations presented in the VCS-PD. In
general context, deforestation rates in the Amazon can be
expected to increase even more compared to those currently
observed in the region. Among the main factors for increased
deforestation after 2018, with an expectation of deforestation
rates even higher than those observed historically so far, the
following factors can be cited: i) Trends in Brazilian legislation:
there are several indications of loosening of environmental
legislation in recent years, mainly in the Bolsonaro government
framework; ii) Increased demand for beef in the international
market.

the emissions from planned deforestation + emissions from
planned logging activities + emissions from planned fuel-wood
and charcoal activities. For this project, the only source of

multiplying the detected areas of planned deforestation with the
carbon stock change per unit area.

4.3 Monitoring Plan
This Monitoring Plan was developed according to Methodology VM0015 “Methodology for
Avoided Unplanned Deforestation”, Version 1.1.3 The methodology encompasses three main
monitoring tasks:
i) Monitoring of actual carbon stock changes and GHG emissions within the project area;
ii) Monitoring of leakage; and
iii) Ex post calculation of net anthropogenic GHG emission reduction.
This Monitoring Plan describes how these tasks will be implemented. For each task, the
monitoring plan includes the following aspects:
a) Technical description of the monitoring tasks.
b) Data to be collected.
c) Overview of data collection procedures.
d) Quality control and quality assurance procedures.
e) Data archiving.
f) Organization and responsibilities of the parties involved in all the above.
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
Figure 9. General overview of parties involved in monitoring activities
Figure 10. General overview of staff in the Project Area
4.3.1 Monitoring of actual carbon stock changes and GHG emissions within the project area
Monitoring of project implementation
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
a) Technical description of the monitoring tasks.
Project activities implemented within the project area will be monitored to be consistent with the
management plans of the project area and the PD.
b) Data to be collected.
Monitoring of deforestation-avoidance activities will be performed by means of evaluations of the
surveillance rounds, and using satellite imagery to continuously inspect the forest condition
within the Project Area. All images, maps and records generated during project implementation
should be conserved and made available to VCS verifiers at verification for inspection to
demonstrate that the AUD project activity has actually been implemented.
Monitoring of social and biodiversity parameters of project implementation will be based on data required
in
Table 10
Table 10 and Table 11, which attest that foreseen activities are being effectively implemented. A
system will be implemented to record data on costs and investments, based on invoices, receipts
and contracts related to activity implementation, as well as signed attendance lists.
c) Overview of data collection procedures.
All invoices, receipts and contracts related to activities implemented in this REDD project shall
be conserved in printed version. Whenever possible, documentation should be kept in electronic
format.
d) Quality control and quality assurance procedures.
The project proponent will train personnel to collect and keep all documentation in a sure place.
All electronic documentation should also be sent to CARBONEXT to further security and checking.
e) Data archiving.
All maps and records generated during project implementation will be conserved and made
available to VCS verifiers at verification. Backup copies of files should be available in the project
proponent facilities, as well as in CARBONEXT facilities.
All documents and records will be kept in a secure retrievable manner for at least two years after
the end of the project crediting period.
f) Organization and responsibilities of the parties involved in all the above.
The project proponent is responsible to implement this monitoring item. CARBONEXT is co-
responsible in archiving, and quality control and quality assurance procedures.
Monitoring of land-use and land-cover change within the project area
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
a) Technical description of the monitoring tasks.
Monitoring of land-use and land-cover change within the Project Area will be performed annually
by analyzing satellite imagery of the Project Area.
Imagery analysis of land use will be based on secondary information from PRODES Digital (INPE,
2014), referring mainly to the classes "Forest Land" and "Non-Forest Land".
b) Data to be collected.
The categories of change that will be subject to MRV-A (monitoring, reporting, verification and
accounting) are “Area of forest land converted to non-forest land”, “Area of forest land undergoing
carbon stock decrease” and “Area of forest land undergoing carbon stock increase”. These
categories are mandatory in AUD project activities having the same characteristics as this project
(i.e., planned logging above the baseline and claiming carbon credits for carbon stock increase).
In this context, data to be collected consists of annual satellite imagery processed by Mapbiomas
or PRODES, if available, for the entire land coverage of Project Area.
The satellite images used will be four scenes of satellite Landsat 8 TM (orbits 001/66, 001/67,
233/66, and 233/67) to the total geographic scope of the Project Area and Reference Region.
c) Overview of data collection procedures.
The project area is located within a region that is subject to a national LU/LC monitoring program
such as Mapbiomas and PRODES. The data generated by those programs are able to be used in
this project. Both sources are applicable for use in this project, according to the criteria listed
below (Methodology VM0015).
i) monitoring occurs in the entire project area and leakage belt.
ii) monitoring occurs in the entire reference region and covers the beginning,
middle and end of the fixed baseline period.
iii) monitors conversion of forest land to non-forest land.
iv) monitoring occurred during the entire fixed baseline period.
v) data accuracies are acceptable according to the methodology criteria.
d) Quality control and quality assurance procedures.
The validation of land-use data used for modeling of land use will be performed by using the
confusion matrix, in order to calculate the overall index of success by period and by class. Three
specific classes will be used: forest, accumulated deforestation and other (hydrography, not
forest, clouds, roads, residues, unclassified objects, and others).
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
The validation will be performed by using be four scenes of satellite Landsat 8 TM (orbits 001/66,
001/67, 233/66, and 233/67) to the total geographic scope of the Project Area and Reference
Region.
With the help of the "Create Random Points" tool in ArcGIS 10.0, 100 random points will be
generated for each class / year as samples for evaluation, using satellite images as reference,
making it possible to generate a confusion matrix for calculation of the accuracy indexes, and
the Kappa index (indicators for validation of mapping accuracy).
Land use classes must have higher values than 90% accuracy for the accuracy and Kappa index,
as required in VM00015 1.1.3 methodology.
e) Data archiving.
All maps and records generated during project implementation will be conserved and made
available to VCS verifiers at verification for inspection to demonstrate that the AUD project activity
has actually been implemented.
All documents and records will be kept in a secure retrievable manner for at least two years after
the end of the project crediting period.
f) Organization and responsibilities of the parties involved in all the above.
All satellite imagery assessments will be performed by AVIX. CARBONEXT is responsible for
reporting and data archiving, according to VM0015, and for providing assistance during
verification audits. The Project Proponent is co-responsible for data archiving.
Monitoring of carbon stock changes and non-CO2 emissions from forest fires
a) Technical description of the monitoring tasks.
Monitoring of carbon stocks is mandatory in the following cases:
Within the project area:
a) Areas subject to significant carbon stock decrease in the project scenario
according to the ex-ante assessment. These will be areas subject to controlled
deforestation and planned harvest activities, such as logging, fuel wood
collection and charcoal production. In these areas, carbon stock changes must
be estimated at least once after each harvest event.
b) Areas subject to unplanned and significant carbon stock decrease, e.g., due
to uncontrolled forest fires and other catastrophic events. In these areas, carbon
stock losses must be estimated as soon as possible after the catastrophic event.
Within leakage management areas:
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
a) Areas subject to planned and significant carbon stock decrease in the project
scenario according to the ex-ante assessment. In these areas, carbon stocks
must be estimated at least once after the planned event that caused the carbon
stock decrease.

4.3.2 Monitoring of Leakage
a) Technical description of the monitoring tasks.
The sources of leakage identified as significant in the ex-ante assessment are subject to
monitoring. Two sources of leakage are potentially subject to monitoring:
- Decrease in carbon stocks and increase in GHG emissions associated with
leakage prevention activities;
- Decrease in carbon stocks and increase in GHG emissions in due to activity
displacement leakage.
This Project Activity does not involve decrease in carbon stocks and increase in GHG emissions
associated with leakage prevention activities. In this project, leakage prevention activities do not
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
involve any carbon stock reduction due to deforestation or additional emissions caused by
increased grazing activities. In this case, only the decrease in carbon stocks and increase in GHG
emissions in due to activity displacement leakage will be monitored.
b) Data to be collected.
Deforestation above the baseline in the leakage belt area will be considered activity
displacement leakage.
The result of the ex-post estimations of carbon stock changes must be reported using the same
table formats used in the ex-ante assessment of baseline carbon stock changes in the leakage
belt.
Table 22.c.1. Ex post above-ground net carbon stock changes in the leakage belt.
Table 22.c.2. Ex post below-ground net carbon stock changes in the leakage belt.
Table 22.c.6. Ex post net carbon stock changes in the wood products in the
leakage belt.
Where strong evidence can be collected that deforestation in the leakage belt is attributable to
deforestation agents that are not linked to the Project Area, the detected deforestation will not
be attributed to the Project Activity and considered leakage. The operational entity verifying the
monitoring data shall determine whether the documentation provided by the project proponent
represents sufficient evidence to consider the detected deforestation as not attributable to the
Project Activity and therefore not leakage.
To estimate the increased GHG emissions due to forest fires in the leakage belt area the
assumption is made that forest clearing is done by burning the forest. The parameter values used
to estimate emissions shall be the same used for estimating forest fires in the baseline (table
23), except for the initial carbon stocks (Cab, Cdw) which shall be those of the initial forest
classes burned in the leakage belt area.

4.3.5 Revisiting the baseline projections for future fixed baseline period
According to VM0015, the baseline will be revisited every 10 years. Thus, the first revision of the
baseline is scheduled for 2028. For this purpose, the following tasks will be carried out:
• Updating information on agents, drivers and underlying causes of deforestation,
which involves the following sub-tasks:
- Collecting information that is relevant to understand deforestation
agents, drivers and underlying causes;
- Redoing step 3 of the ex-ante methodology, as specified in the
methodology;
- Recalibrating the model for projection of future deforestation, using new
“Factor Maps” for the subsequent fixed baseline period.
• Adjusting the land-use and land-cover change component of the baseline, which
involves reassessing the following components of the baseline projections:
- The annual areas of baseline deforestation.
- The location of baseline deforestation.
- Adjustment of the annual areas of baseline deforestation.
- Adjustment of the location of the projected baseline deforestation.
• Adjusting, as needed, the carbon component of the baseline (this task will only
be carried out if more accurate methods for carbon stocks estimates are
available in the occasion of baseline revision).

To project the LU/LC classes that will replace forests in the baseline case, Method 1 “Historical
LU/LC-change” was chosen, because historical LU/LC-changes are assumed to be representative
of future trends. Hence, post-deforestation land-uses are allocated to the projected areas of
annual deforestation in same proportions as those observed on lands deforested during the
historical reference period in the Reference Region.
As mentioned in “1.13 Conditions Prior to Project Initiation” of this VCS-PD, pasture accounts for
virtually all the deforested land occupation in the project region. An analysis of land-use cover in
Lábrea Municipality over the historical reference period years16, show that, of the agricultural
land uses, pastureland accounts for over 99% of the area in every year analysed. Corresponding
evidence was duly presented at audit, under category “16 – Land use land cover maps”. Thus,
the post deforestation class is considered exclusively as “pasture”.

Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
Conversion of forest to non-forest involving fires is a source of emissions of non-CO2 gases (CH4
and N2O). Sources report a steady increase in the number of fire alerts in Lábrea over the
historical reference period. Furthermore, GIS analysis was conducted utilizing data on fire
occurrence from the CPTEC/INPE database17 and Mapbiomas18.
To estimate non-CO2 emissions from forest fires, the average percentage of the deforested area
in which fire was used, the average proportion of mass burnt in each carbon pool (Pburnt,p), and
the average combustion efficiency of each pool (CEp) were estimated. These average percentage
values were estimated for each forest class (icl) and are assumed to remain the same in the
future.
In accordance with the VM0015 methodology, GHG emissions from biomass burning were
estimated based on revised IPCC 1996 GL LULUCF guidance, as follows.

Ex-ante estimation of actual carbon stock changes
The ex-post carbon stock changes during the present monitoring period are due to the
following:
- Planned activities forest management within the project area.
- Unplanned deforestation that cannot be avoided.
Carbon stock changes due to possible future catastrophic events cannot be predicted and were
therefore excluded from the ex-ante assessment.
Certain discrete areas of forest within the project area will be subject to project activities that will
change the carbon stocks of these areas compared to the baseline. In this Project Activity, such
activities are related to planned timber logging.
According to the Sustainable Forest Management Plan, the annual area of sustainable logging is
3,931.62 hectares. Given that the total management area is of 43,247.79 hectares, it is assumed
that logging activities will be carried out over 11 years, followed by 15 years of fallow period. The
table below shows the yearly estimates of carbon stock decrease due to planned logging activities
in the Project Area.
The increase following planned logging activities in the Project Area has also been estimated,
using secondary data by West et al. (2014), which indicates an annual increase of 3.03 tCO2 per
hectare per year due to post-logging regeneration of vegetation.

Some unplanned deforestation may happen in the project area despite the AUD project activity.
The level at which deforestation will actually be reduced in the project case depends on the
effectiveness of the proposed activities, which cannot be measured ex ante.
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
To allow ex ante projections to be made, the project proponent shall make a conservative
assumption about the effectiveness of the proposed project activities and estimate an
Effectiveness Index (EI) between 0 (no effectiveness) and 1 (maximum effectiveness). The
estimated value of EI is used to multiply the baseline projections by the factor (1 - EI) and the
result shall be considered the ex-ante estimated emissions from unplanned deforestation in the
project case. The project design team conservatively considers that surveillance activities are
able to attain 90% of effectiveness in avoiding unplanned deforestation inside the Project Area.
According to studies comparing the incidence of fires in the Reference Region and the Project
Area (for 2019 and 2020), it is evidenced the initial fire occurrences in PA are 74% lower than in
the RR. Moreover, only three of these occurrences resulted in biomass loss, as demonstrated by
satellite imagery. Evidence is available to auditors.

emissions by the factor (1 – EI).

Leakage
Activities that will cause deforestation within the project area in the baseline case could be
displaced outside the project boundary due to the implementation of the AUD project activity. If
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
carbon stocks in the leakage belt area will decrease more during project implementation than
projected in the baseline case, this will be an indication that leakage due to displacement of
baseline activities has occurred. Leakage due to activity displacement can thus be estimated by
ex post monitoring of deforestation in the leakage belt and comparing ex post observed
deforestation with ex ante projected baseline deforestation.
Ex ante activity displacement leakage can only be guessed based on the anticipated combined
effectiveness of the proposed leakage prevention measures and project activities. This shall be
done by multiplying the estimated baseline carbon stock changes for the project area by a
“Displacement Leakage Factor” (DLF) representing the percent of deforestation expected to be
displaced outside the project boundary.
If deforestation agents do not participate in leakage prevention activities and project activities,
the Displacement Factor shall be 100%. Where leakage prevention activities are implemented,
the factor shall be equal to the proportion of the baseline agents estimated to be given the
opportunity to participate in leakage prevention activities and project activities.
It is expected that 100% of potential deforestation agents in the Reference Region will be given
the opportunity to participate in leakage prevention activities. Thus, the “Displacement Leakage
Factor” (DLF) is conservatively considered as 0.05.
If emissions from forest fires have been included in the baseline, the ex-ante emissions from
forest fires due to activity displacement leakage will be calculated by multiplying baseline forest
fire emissions in the project area by the same DLF used to estimate the decrease in carbon
stocks.

Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals
The determination of which GHG emissions by sources, decreases in carbon pools, and leakage
emissions are significant for this Project Activity has been carried out using the “Tool for testing
significance of GHG emissions in A/R CDM project activities (Version 01)”. According to the tool,
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
the GHG emissions by sources, decreases in carbon pools and leakage emissions are all
considered significant, as their sums represent more than 5% of net emission reductions.

5.3 Project Emissions
The use of carbon stock estimates in similar ecosystems derived from local studies, literature
and IPCC defaults is permitted, provided the accuracy and conservativeness of the estimates are
demonstrated. Data presented in the next table have been calculated from this scientific
publication, as well as superior and inferior limits of the 90% Confidence Intervals.
Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4.0
Table 35 Ex post carbon stock per hectare of initial forest classes icl existing in the project area
and leakage belt (Table 15a of VM0015)
The 90% Confidence Intervals were used to define if the most suitable choice would be the
average or the inferior limit of intervals, to mitigate uncertainties in estimates, as shown in the
table below. In the present case, the averages of above and below-ground biomass have been
utilized for calculation; while the superior limit of the interval has been taken for the calculations
related to the wood products carbon pool, for conservativeness purposes and mitigation of
uncertainties.
Table 36. Ex post carbon stock per hectare of initial forest classes icl existing in the project

classes, for which the upper limit of the interval was taken in the case of above-ground
biomass, while the average was utilized for below-ground biomass, for conservativeness
purposes and mitigation of uncertainties. However, in the case of project emissions, the lower
confidence intervals were utilized for above- and below-ground biomass, for conservativeness
purposes.

According to land use classification up to July 2021, there was 1027.5 ha of unplanned
deforestation within the Project Area over the 2018 – 2021 period, as demonstrated in the table
and figure below. Although project fire brigade training had taken place on 9 March 2019, these
measures and actions were ineffectual in mitigating fires within the project on this occasion. This
failure is due to the recent implementation of fire brigade measures as well as the aggressive
nature of the invasion, evidenced by the police report made available at audit, meaning that entry
of project staff to the area was not possible. Furthermore, frequent remote monitoring of the area
was not available at the time, as this is on track for implementation in 2022.
